content.text: What It Is

API contract documentation is the practice of formally specifying, in a machine-readable and human-readable format, the exact interface that an API exposes — what it accepts, what it returns, what errors it can produce, and what behaviors consumers can rely on. In the context of agentic AI systems, an API contract covers more than just REST endpoints: it encompasses every tool an agent can call, every event it can emit or consume, every message schema it exchanges with other agents or orchestrators, and every constraint on those interactions.

A contract is distinct from a description. A description tells a developer what an API does in prose. A contract is a formal specification that can be parsed by tools, validated against at runtime, used to generate client code, and checked for backward compatibility by a CI pipeline. When an agent's tool definitions are expressed as OpenAPI specifications, JSON Schema documents, or AsyncAPI specs, they become contracts that the entire engineering organization — including other agents — can reason about systematically.

Agentic systems have unique contract documentation needs because agents interact with APIs differently than traditional software. An LLM-based agent must parse a tool's description and parameter schema at inference time to decide whether and how to invoke it. A poorly specified contract degrades agent reasoning quality, causes incorrect tool calls, and makes debugging opaque. A well-specified contract, on the other hand, becomes a first-class engineering artifact that drives not just documentation but also validation, testing, code generation, and versioning.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI systems are composite: a single user request may require an orchestrating agent to invoke five or ten tools, each of which may itself call downstream services. Without formal contracts at each boundary, this composability becomes fragile. Any change to a tool's parameter names, types, or return shape can silently break the agent's ability to call it correctly, produce hallucinated arguments that fail schema validation, or cause cascading failures through a chain of agent invocations.

The stakes are higher in agentic systems than in conventional software for several reasons. First, LLMs use the tool schema and description to generate structured arguments. Ambiguity in a parameter name or a missing
description

field in a JSON Schema doesn't just confuse a human developer — it degrades the model's ability to invoke the tool correctly, often without any error message, just silent misbehavior. Second, agentic pipelines are often partially or fully autonomous, meaning bugs may not be caught until they've propagated through many steps. Third, multi-agent architectures often span team boundaries; without a stable contract, teams cannot develop and deploy their components independently.

API contract documentation also matters for observability. When every tool call and event emission is governed by a versioned, machine-readable schema, monitoring systems can validate payloads in real time, detect schema drift, and alert on unexpected data shapes before they cause downstream failures. Without contracts, each agent boundary is a blind spot.
Benefits
Improved agent reasoning quality:
When tool schemas include rich descriptions, examples, and constraints, LLMs produce more accurate tool invocations, reducing wasted tokens and failed calls.
Automated validation:
Contracts enable runtime validation of every tool call argument and return value against the declared schema, catching type mismatches and missing fields before they propagate.
Client code generation:
OpenAPI and AsyncAPI specs can generate typed client SDKs in multiple languages, eliminating manual boilerplate and ensuring clients always match the server's interface.
Independent team development:
With a stable contract, the team building an agent tool and the team integrating it can work in parallel. The contract is the handshake that removes scheduling dependencies.
Breaking-change detection:
Automated diffing tools (e.g.,
openapi-diff
bump.sh
) can compare versions of a spec and flag breaking changes in CI, preventing accidental regressions.
Documentation portal generation:
Tools like Swagger UI, Redoc, and AsyncAPI Studio render specifications as interactive documentation portals automatically, so documentation stays in sync with the code.
Consumer-driven contract testing:

Frameworks like Pact allow consumers (agents that call tools) to define the subset of the interface they use. Providers (tool implementations) run these consumer-defined tests to verify compatibility without end-to-end integration tests.
Trade-offs

API contract documentation is not free. The primary cost is the discipline required to keep contracts synchronized with implementations. A spec that drifts from the actual behavior is worse than no spec, because it misleads consumers, causes tests to pass against the wrong contract, and erodes trust in the documentation system.

The "specification-first" approach (write the spec, then implement) maximizes the benefits of contracts but requires teams to invest upfront design time and to resist the temptation to start coding immediately. The "code-first" approach (generate specs from code annotations) is more natural for many developers but produces specifications that reflect implementation accidents rather than intentional design. The right balance depends on the team's maturity and the stability of the API.

Contracts also introduce a versioning obligation. Once consumers depend on a contract, you cannot change it freely. Adding fields is usually safe; removing or renaming them is breaking. This disciplined versioning is the correct engineering behavior but requires process changes that teams sometimes resist. Finally, some agentic tool interactions are genuinely hard to specify formally: tools that return semi-structured natural language, tools whose output schema varies by input, or tools with side effects that are difficult to capture in a request/response model may require pragmatic workarounds in the specification.
How It Works
API contract documentation in agentic systems works at three layers: the tool schema layer, the service interface layer, and the event/message layer.
At the
tool schema layer

, each tool the agent can call is described by a JSON Schema document that specifies its parameters, their types, which are required, and what values are valid. This schema is embedded in the agent's system prompt or tool registry and is used by the LLM at inference time to construct structured function calls. The schema must be precise enough to guide the model but concise enough to fit in the context window.
At the
service interface layer

, tools that expose HTTP APIs are documented as OpenAPI 3.x specifications. These specs describe every endpoint, path parameter, query parameter, request body schema, and response schema. They are the source of truth for client code generation, automated contract testing, and the documentation portal.
At the
event/message layer

, agents that communicate asynchronously through message queues, pub/sub systems, or streaming channels are documented with AsyncAPI specs. AsyncAPI extends the OpenAPI mental model to event-driven architectures: it describes channels (topics, queues), the operations that publish or subscribe to them, and the message schemas for each channel. This is essential for multi-agent systems where agents emit events that trigger other agents.

The contracts at all three layers feed into a shared documentation portal (e.g., Swagger UI, Redoc, or AsyncAPI Studio), a contract test suite (e.g., Pact, Dredd, or custom schema validation), and a CI/CD pipeline that checks for breaking changes on every pull request. A version registry tracks the history of each contract, enabling rollback and compatibility checking across deployed versions.
Architecture
The diagram below illustrates how API contract documentation flows through an agentic system, from initial tool definitions through validation and into a versioned registry.

The flow has five major stages. First, developers define agent tool interfaces — the parameters an agent can pass and the responses it can receive. These definitions are expressed as JSON Schema embedded in tool registration code. Second, a specification layer translates these definitions into formal OpenAPI (for synchronous HTTP tools) or AsyncAPI (for event-driven channels) specs, which are the primary artifacts for downstream consumers. Third, a documentation portal (Redoc, Swagger UI, AsyncAPI Studio) renders the specs into interactive, browsable documentation that is auto-rebuilt on every spec change. Fourth, the specs drive two parallel tracks: client code generation (typed SDKs and stubs for agents to call tools) and consumer-driven contract tests (Pact interactions that providers must satisfy). Fifth, all these artifacts flow through a CI/CD pipeline that validates schemas, runs contract tests, diffs new specs against previous versions to detect breaking changes, and, on success, publishes the new spec version to the version registry. The registry feeds back into the agent tool definitions cycle when breaking changes require a new major version.
When to Use It

Formal API contract documentation is appropriate for any tool or API boundary that is consumed by more than one agent, more than one team, or in an autonomous pipeline where failures are hard to detect and debug. Specifically:

Use OpenAPI contracts whenever an agent tool is exposed via HTTP and will be called by LLM-generated function calls. Even single-agent, single-team projects benefit from the validation and documentation this provides.

Use AsyncAPI contracts for any event-driven communication between agents: task queues, result streams, state change notifications, or pub/sub channels used by an orchestrating agent to coordinate sub-agents.

Use consumer-driven contracts (Pact) whenever the team building a tool and the team consuming it (the agent) need to develop in parallel without a shared test environment. This is especially important in microservice architectures.

Invest in a documentation portal whenever the system has more than two or three tools, or when the tools are consumed by agents developed by different people. The portal becomes a discovery mechanism for what tools exist and how to use them.

Use versioning strategies from the first public release of any tool API. Retrofitting versioning onto an already-deployed, widely-consumed API is significantly harder than building it in from the start.

Lightweight JSON Schema documentation (without the full OpenAPI scaffolding) may be sufficient for purely internal, single-team tool registries where the tool definitions live in the same repository as the agent code. However, even in this case, the schema should include
description
fields on every parameter, because the LLM reads these descriptions to reason about how to invoke the tool.
How to Implement
Implementation starts with choosing a specification format and a code-first vs. spec-first workflow, then wiring the spec into CI and the documentation portal.

Below is a complete OpenAPI 3.1 specification for a web-search agent tool. This illustrates the level of detail a well-specified agent tool contract requires: descriptions for every field, explicit type constraints, a structured response schema, and error responses the agent must handle.

openapi: "3.1.0"
info:
  title: Agent Web Search Tool API
  version: "1.2.0"
  description: |
    HTTP interface for the web_search tool used by agentic AI systems.
    Callers are LLM agents that construct arguments from JSON Schema at
    inference time. All descriptions are agent-facing: write them to
    guide model reasoning, not human developers.
  contact:
    name: Platform Team
    email: platform@example.com

servers:
  - url: https://tools.example.com/v1
    description: Production tool server
  - url: https://tools-staging.example.com/v1
    description: Staging environment

paths:
  /search:
    post:
      operationId: webSearch
      summary: Search the web and return ranked results
      description: |
        Executes a web search using the provided query and returns a list
        of ranked results with titles, URLs, and content snippets.
        Use this tool when you need current information not in your training
        data. Prefer specific, factual queries over vague ones.
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/SearchRequest"
            examples:
              factual_query:
                summary: Factual lookup
                value:
                  query: "Python 3.12 release date"
                  max_results: 5
                  safe_search: true
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Successful search response
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/SearchResponse"
        "400":
          description: Invalid request (empty query, out-of-range max_results)
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/ErrorResponse"
        "429":
          description: Rate limit exceeded
          headers:
            Retry-After:
              schema:
                type: integer
              description: Seconds until rate limit resets
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/ErrorResponse"
        "503":
          description: Search backend unavailable
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/ErrorResponse"

components:
  schemas:
    SearchRequest:
      type: object
      required:
        - query
      description: Parameters for a web search invocation.
      properties:
        query:
          type: string
          minLength: 1
          maxLength: 500
          description: |
            The search query string. Write this as you would type it into
            a search engine. For best results, use specific nouns and avoid
            stop words. Example: "climate change CO2 levels 2024".
        max_results:
          type: integer
          minimum: 1
          maximum: 20
          default: 5
          description: |
            Maximum number of results to return. Higher values increase
            latency. Use 3-5 for most tasks; increase only when you need
            to survey a broad topic.
        safe_search:
          type: boolean
          default: true
          description: |
            When true, filters adult content from results. Should remain
            true for all production agent tasks.
        language:
          type: string
          pattern: "^[a-z]{2}(-[A-Z]{2})?$"
          default: "en"
          description: |
            BCP-47 language code for search results. Default is English.
            Example values: "en", "fr", "de", "zh-CN".

    SearchResponse:
      type: object
      required:
        - results
        - total_results
        - query_time_ms
      properties:
        results:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/SearchResult"
          description: Ordered list of search results, best match first.
        total_results:
          type: integer
          description: Estimated total number of results for the query.
        query_time_ms:
          type: integer
          description: Server-side time taken to execute the search in milliseconds.

    SearchResult:
      type: object
      required:
        - title
        - url
        - snippet
      properties:
        title:
          type: string
          description: Page title as indexed by the search engine.
        url:
          type: string
          format: uri
          description: Canonical URL of the result page.
        snippet:
          type: string
          description: |
            Short excerpt from the page most relevant to the query.
            May include highlighted terms. Use this to assess relevance
            before deciding whether to fetch the full page.
        published_date:
          type: string
          format: date
          nullable: true
          description: Publication or last-modified date, if available.
        domain:
          type: string
          description: Registered domain of the URL (e.g., "wikipedia.org").

    ErrorResponse:
      type: object
      required:
        - error_code
        - message
      properties:
        error_code:
          type: string
          enum:
            - INVALID_QUERY
            - RATE_LIMITED
            - BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE
            - UNAUTHORIZED
          description: Machine-readable error code for programmatic handling.
        message:
          type: string
          description: Human-readable error description.
        retry_after_seconds:
          type: integer
          nullable: true
          description: Present only for RATE_LIMITED errors.

  securitySchemes:
    ApiKeyAuth:
      type: apiKey
      in: header
      name: X-Tool-API-Key
      description: API key issued per agent deployment.

security:
  - ApiKeyAuth: []
Below is an AsyncAPI 3.0 specification for the event-driven communication pattern where an orchestrating agent publishes task assignments and sub-agents publish completion events:

asyncapi: "3.0.0"
info:
  title: Agent Task Queue
  version: "2.0.0"
  description: |
    AsyncAPI specification for the task assignment and completion
    event streams used to coordinate sub-agents in the pipeline.
    The orchestrator publishes to task.assigned; sub-agents publish
    to task.completed and task.failed.

servers:
  production:
    host: broker.example.com:9092
    protocol: kafka
    description: Production Kafka cluster
  development:
    host: localhost:9092
    protocol: kafka
    description: Local development broker

channels:
  task/assigned:
    address: task.assigned
    description: |
      Orchestrator publishes a message here when it assigns a sub-task
      to a sub-agent. Sub-agents subscribe to receive work.
    messages:
      TaskAssigned:
        $ref: "#/components/messages/TaskAssigned"

  task/completed:
    address: task.completed
    description: |
      Sub-agents publish here when they finish a task successfully.
      The orchestrator subscribes to aggregate results.
    messages:
      TaskCompleted:
        $ref: "#/components/messages/TaskCompleted"

  task/failed:
    address: task.failed
    description: |
      Sub-agents publish here when a task fails after all retries.
      The orchestrator subscribes to handle failures and retries.
    messages:
      TaskFailed:
        $ref: "#/components/messages/TaskFailed"

operations:
  publishTaskAssigned:
    action: send
    channel:
      $ref: "#/channels/task~1assigned"
    summary: Orchestrator assigns a task to a sub-agent
    description: |
      The orchestrating agent sends this after planning to dispatch
      a discrete unit of work. Include all context the sub-agent needs;
      sub-agents do not have access to prior conversation history.

  consumeTaskCompleted:
    action: receive
    channel:
      $ref: "#/channels/task~1completed"
    summary: Orchestrator receives task completion

  consumeTaskFailed:
    action: receive
    channel:
      $ref: "#/channels/task~1failed"
    summary: Orchestrator receives task failure notification

components:
  messages:
    TaskAssigned:
      name: TaskAssigned
      title: Task Assignment
      contentType: application/json
      payload:
        $ref: "#/components/schemas/TaskAssignedPayload"
      headers:
        type: object
        properties:
          correlation-id:
            type: string
            format: uuid
            description: Trace ID to correlate task events in observability.
          schema-version:
            type: string
            description: Payload schema version (e.g., "2.0").

    TaskCompleted:
      name: TaskCompleted
      title: Task Completion
      contentType: application/json
      payload:
        $ref: "#/components/schemas/TaskCompletedPayload"

    TaskFailed:
      name: TaskFailed
      title: Task Failure
      contentType: application/json
      payload:
        $ref: "#/components/schemas/TaskFailedPayload"

  schemas:
    TaskAssignedPayload:
      type: object
      required:
        - task_id
        - task_type
        - assigned_at
        - context
      properties:
        task_id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
          description: Unique identifier for this task instance.
        task_type:
          type: string
          enum:
            - web_research
            - code_generation
            - data_analysis
            - document_summarization
          description: Determines which sub-agent type should consume this task.
        assigned_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          description: ISO 8601 timestamp when the orchestrator assigned the task.
        context:
          type: object
          description: |
            Arbitrary key-value context the sub-agent needs. Schema varies
            by task_type — see the task-specific schema extensions.
          additionalProperties: true
        deadline:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          nullable: true
          description: Latest time by which the task must complete.
        priority:
          type: integer
          minimum: 1
          maximum: 5
          default: 3
          description: "Task priority: 1=lowest, 5=highest."

    TaskCompletedPayload:
      type: object
      required:
        - task_id
        - completed_at
        - result
      properties:
        task_id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        completed_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        result:
          type: object
          description: The task output, schema determined by task_type.
          additionalProperties: true
        tokens_used:
          type: integer
          description: Total LLM tokens consumed during task execution.

    TaskFailedPayload:
      type: object
      required:
        - task_id
        - failed_at
        - error_code
        - message
      properties:
        task_id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        failed_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        error_code:
          type: string
          enum:
            - TOOL_TIMEOUT
            - SCHEMA_VALIDATION_FAILED
            - LLM_REFUSED
            - MAX_RETRIES_EXCEEDED
            - CONTEXT_LIMIT_EXCEEDED
        message:
          type: string
        retry_count:
          type: integer
          description: Number of attempts made before giving up.
Examples
This example shows how to generate an OpenAPI spec from Python code using
FastAPI
and
pydantic
, so the spec is always synchronized with the implementation. The docstrings and field descriptions become part of the generated OpenAPI document.

from datetime import date
from typing import Optional
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
import uvicorn

app = FastAPI(
    title="Agent Web Search Tool API",
    version="1.2.0",
    description=(
        "HTTP interface for the web_search tool used by agentic AI systems. "
        "All descriptions guide model reasoning at inference time."
    ),
    contact={"name": "Platform Team", "email": "platform@example.com"},
)


class SearchRequest(BaseModel):
    """Parameters for a web search invocation."""

    query: str = Field(
        ...,
        min_length=1,
        max_length=500,
        description=(
            "The search query string. Write this as you would type it into "
            "a search engine. Use specific nouns; avoid stop words. "
            "Example: 'climate change CO2 levels 2024'."
        ),
    )
    max_results: int = Field(
        default=5,
        ge=1,
        le=20,
        description=(
            "Maximum number of results to return. Use 3-5 for most tasks; "
            "increase only when surveying a broad topic."
        ),
    )
    safe_search: bool = Field(
        default=True,
        description="Filters adult content when true. Keep true for production agents.",
    )
    language: str = Field(
        default="en",
        pattern=r"^[a-z]{2}(-[A-Z]{2})?$",
        description="BCP-47 language code. Default is English ('en').",
    )


class SearchResult(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="Page title as indexed.")
    url: str = Field(description="Canonical URL of the result page.")
    snippet: str = Field(
        description=(
            "Short excerpt most relevant to the query. Use this to assess "
            "relevance before fetching the full page."
        )
    )
    published_date: Optional[date] = Field(
        default=None, description="Publication date, if available."
    )
    domain: str = Field(description="Registered domain (e.g., 'wikipedia.org').")


class SearchResponse(BaseModel):
    results: list[SearchResult] = Field(
        description="Ordered list of search results, best match first."
    )
    total_results: int = Field(
        description="Estimated total number of results for the query."
    )
    query_time_ms: int = Field(
        description="Server-side search execution time in milliseconds."
    )


class ErrorResponse(BaseModel):
    error_code: str = Field(
        description="Machine-readable error code for programmatic handling."
    )
    message: str = Field(description="Human-readable error description.")


@app.post(
    "/search",
    response_model=SearchResponse,
    operation_id="webSearch",
    summary="Search the web and return ranked results",
    description=(
        "Executes a web search and returns ranked results with titles, URLs, "
        "and snippets. Use when you need current information not in training data."
    ),
    responses={
        400: {"model": ErrorResponse, "description": "Invalid request"},
        429: {"model": ErrorResponse, "description": "Rate limit exceeded"},
    },
)
async def web_search(request: SearchRequest) -> SearchResponse:
    # Implementation omitted — FastAPI generates the OpenAPI spec from
    # the type annotations and Field() descriptions above.
    ...


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # The OpenAPI spec is available at /openapi.json
    # Interactive docs are at /docs (Swagger UI) and /redoc (Redoc)
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

This example shows a Pact consumer test — a test written from the agent's perspective that defines exactly what it expects the tool API to return, and can be run against the provider to verify compatibility without a live integration environment:

# consumer_pact_test.py
# Run: pytest consumer_pact_test.py

import httpx
import pytest
from pact import Consumer, Provider

pact = Consumer("SearchAgent").has_pact_with(
    Provider("WebSearchTool"),
    pact_dir="./pacts",
    publish_to_broker=True,
    broker_base_url="https://pact-broker.example.com",
)


@pytest.fixture(scope="module", autouse=True)
def pact_fixture():
    pact.start_service()
    yield
    pact.stop_service()


def test_successful_search():
    """The SearchAgent expects a 200 with at least one result for a valid query."""
    expected_response = {
        "results": [
            {
                "title": "Python 3.12 Release Notes",
                "url": "https://docs.python.org/3.12/whatsnew/3.12.html",
                "snippet": "Python 3.12 was released on October 2, 2023.",
                "domain": "docs.python.org",
            }
        ],
        "total_results": 42,
        "query_time_ms": 120,
    }

    (
        pact.given("the search backend returns results for 'Python 3.12 release date'")
        .upon_receiving("a search request from SearchAgent")
        .with_request(
            method="POST",
            path="/search",
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body={"query": "Python 3.12 release date", "max_results": 5},
        )
        .will_respond_with(
            status=200,
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body=expected_response,
        )
    )

    with pact:
        response = httpx.post(
            pact.uri + "/search",
            json={"query": "Python 3.12 release date", "max_results": 5},
        )
        assert response.status_code == 200
        data = response.json()
        assert len(data["results"]) >= 1
        assert data["results"][0]["url"].startswith("https://")
Real-World Example

Consider a production agentic research assistant at a financial services firm. The assistant can invoke several tools: a market data API, a document retrieval API, a news search API, and a calculation engine. The firm's platform team initially built these tools ad hoc, with informal README documentation and no formal schemas. Over six months, they observed three recurring problems.

First, the LLM frequently passed date parameters in the wrong format — sometimes ISO 8601, sometimes MM/DD/YYYY — because the tool's description did not specify the expected format. The market data API silently accepted some formats and rejected others, producing inconsistent data with no clear error. Second, the document retrieval API's team changed the name of the
query
parameter to
search_query

in a hotfix deployment. The agent began silently failing to pass the query — the parameter was ignored rather than causing a 400 — and returning empty results for three days before anyone noticed. Third, the calculation engine returned numeric results as strings in some code paths and as floats in others, causing the agent to occasionally misparse values.
The platform team's remediation was to mandate OpenAPI 3.1 specifications for every tool. They added an
openapi-diff
step in CI that blocked any pull request that changed an existing field's name, type, or required status without bumping the major version. They required that every parameter include a
description
and an
example
. They deployed Pact and wrote consumer tests from the agent's perspective for every tool call the agent made in its ten most common workflows.
Within two months, all three recurring failure modes had disappeared from production. The most significant improvement was in the LLM's tool invocation quality: with richer
description
and
example
fields in the JSON Schema, the model's rate of correctly-formatted tool calls increased from 84% to 97%, as measured by schema validation middleware on the tool server.
Common Mistakes
Omitting descriptions on parameters:
The most impactful mistake. LLMs use the
description
field to reason about what value to supply. An undescribed parameter named
mode
gives the model no guidance; a described one guides it precisely.
Writing spec descriptions for human developers instead of LLMs:
"See the internal wiki" or "Deprecated, use v2" are useless to a model at inference time. Descriptions should contain the actual content the model needs to construct correct arguments.
Treating the spec as documentation-only:
If the spec is not validated against actual requests and responses in CI, it drifts from reality. Integrate schema validation in the tool server's request handling, not just in docs.
Using additionalProperties: true everywhere:
This disables property validation on the schema. Schemas should be as closed as possible — only add
additionalProperties: true
where the schema is genuinely open-ended and document why.
Making breaking changes without versioning:
Renaming a field, making an optional field required, or changing a type are all breaking changes. They must be accompanied by a major version bump and a migration path for consumers.
Splitting contract from implementation across separate repositories without automation:

If the spec lives in a docs repo and the implementation in a service repo, they will diverge. Either generate the spec from code or enforce alignment with a CI check that validates the implementation against the spec.
Skipping error response documentation:
Agents must handle errors from tool calls. An undocumented error shape means the agent has no schema to validate error responses against and may misinterpret them.
Best Practices
Write descriptions for LLM reasoning:
Every parameter and response field should have a
description
that explains its semantics, valid values, format constraints, and examples in agent-facing terms. Include at least one concrete example for non-obvious parameters.
Use semantic versioning (SemVer) explicitly:
Every tool API should carry a
version
field in its spec. Bump major versions for breaking changes, minor for additive changes, patch for fixes. Use URL path versioning (
) for HTTP tool APIs.
Generate specs from code where possible:
Use FastAPI, Pydantic, or similar frameworks where type annotations and Field() descriptions generate the OpenAPI spec automatically. This eliminates the most common cause of spec drift.
Validate specs in CI:
Run
spectral lint
against all OpenAPI/AsyncAPI specs on every pull request. Fail the build on violations of your spec style rules (e.g., missing descriptions, undocumented responses).
Detect breaking changes automatically:
Add
openapi-diff
or
bump.sh
to CI. Block merges that introduce breaking changes to an existing major version without a version bump.
Write consumer-driven contract tests for every critical tool interaction:
The agent's expected interactions with each tool should be captured as Pact interactions. Run provider verification tests on every tool deployment to catch regressions before they reach production.
Keep schemas strict but extensible:
Close schemas with
additionalProperties: false
where you want strict validation. Use
nullable: true
and optional fields to allow forward-compatible extensions.
Performance & Scalability
API contract documentation has performance implications at multiple points in an agentic system's lifecycle.
At
inference time

, the tool schema is embedded in the context window. Overly verbose schemas — schemas with extremely long descriptions, excessive examples, or deeply nested structures — consume tokens that could be used for reasoning. The trade-off is between completeness and context efficiency. A useful heuristic: keep each individual tool's JSON Schema under 800 tokens in its serialized form. Use the minimum description that guides the model correctly, not the maximum that a human reviewer would find thorough. For large tool registries (20+ tools), consider dynamic tool loading that injects only the schemas for tools likely to be relevant to the current task.
At
request time

, runtime schema validation (validating every tool call argument against the JSON Schema) adds latency. For high-throughput agentic pipelines, this can be significant. Strategies include: caching compiled schema validators (do not recompile the schema on every request), using a fast validation library (
jsonschema
in Python is slower than
fastjsonschema
or
pydantic-core
), and performing validation asynchronously in a logging path rather than in the hot path for non-critical checks.
At
CI time

, contract test suites and spec diffing jobs should be fast enough not to block developer iteration. Parallelize Pact provider verification runs across multiple tools. Cache the previous spec version in CI artifacts to speed up diff checks. Run only the contract tests for tools affected by the current pull request using dependency analysis.
At
documentation scale

, a system with hundreds of tools needs a searchable documentation portal, not a single large page. Use a documentation system that supports filtering by tag, version, and tool category. Pre-render static documentation on spec change events rather than dynamically rendering on every page load.
Tooling & Ecosystem
The API contract documentation ecosystem is mature and well-integrated, with tools covering the entire lifecycle from authoring to validation to documentation rendering:
Spectral

: An open-source JSON/YAML linter for OpenAPI and AsyncAPI specs. Define custom rules to enforce your organization's spec style (e.g., all parameters must have descriptions, all responses must be documented). Run as a CI check via
spectral lint openapi.yaml
Swagger UI / Redoc
: Documentation portal generators for OpenAPI specs. Swagger UI is interactive (try-it-out requests); Redoc produces cleaner read-only documentation. FastAPI ships both at
/docs
and
/redoc
by default.
AsyncAPI Studio
: A web-based editor and renderer for AsyncAPI specifications. Provides a visual view of channels, operations, and message schemas, equivalent to Swagger UI for synchronous APIs.
openapi-generator / openapi-typescript-codegen
: Generate typed client SDKs in Python, TypeScript, Go, Java, and many other languages from an OpenAPI spec. Run in CI to regenerate clients on every spec change.
Pact
: Consumer-driven contract testing framework with support for HTTP and message-based interactions. The Pact Broker stores and shares contracts between teams. Available in Python (
pact-python
), TypeScript, Java, and other languages.
Dredd
: Tests an HTTP service against its OpenAPI spec by making real HTTP calls and validating responses. Useful for smoke testing that the implementation matches the spec.
bump.sh / oasdiff
: Breaking change detection for OpenAPI specs. Compare two versions of a spec and get a structured report of breaking changes. Integrate in CI to block breaking changes without a version bump.
FastAPI + Pydantic
: The Python ecosystem's most ergonomic code-first OpenAPI generation stack. Define models with Pydantic; FastAPI generates the spec and serves interactive docs automatically.
jsonschema / pydantic-core / fastjsonschema
: Python libraries for runtime JSON Schema validation.
pydantic-core
(Rust-backed) is the fastest;
jsonschema
is the reference implementation. Use for validating LLM-generated tool arguments before execution.
Comparison with Alternatives

The table below compares the four main specification approaches for documenting agent tool APIs. Each has different strengths depending on the tool's communication pattern and the agent's integration style.
Approach
Best For
Tooling
Versioning Support
Agent Tool Fit
OpenAPI 3.x
Synchronous HTTP/REST tool APIs; request-response tools with typed parameters and response schemas
Excellent — Swagger UI, Redoc, openapi-generator, Spectral, Dredd, oasdiff, Pact HTTP; wide language support
Explicit version in info.version; URL path versioning (/v1/, /v2/) is idiomatic; oasdiff detects breaking changes
Excellent — the de facto standard for HTTP tool APIs in agentic systems; directly drives JSON Schema tool definitions
AsyncAPI 3.x
Event-driven, asynchronous agent communication: task queues, pub/sub channels, streaming result events between agents
Good — AsyncAPI Studio, AsyncAPI Generator, Microcks; growing but smaller ecosystem than OpenAPI
Explicit version in info.version; channel-level versioning possible; no standard breaking-change detector yet
Excellent for multi-agent event streams; not applicable to synchronous tool calls
GraphQL Schema
Tools that need flexible, client-specified query shapes; agents that need to fetch deeply nested or partially structured data
Good — GraphiQL, Altair, graphql-inspector; strong type system; complex for simple request-response tools
Challenging — no official versioning; relies on field deprecation and additive changes; graphql-inspector detects breaking changes
Moderate — useful for data-fetching tools where the agent can specify required fields; overkill for simple action tools
Custom JSON Schema
Agent tool parameter definitions embedded in LLM context; non-HTTP tools; internal tool registries
Basic — jsonschema validators in any language; no documentation portal or client generation without extra tooling
Manual only — versioning must be implemented by the team; no standard diff tooling
Excellent for the tool definition layer (what the LLM sees); insufficient alone for full service-level contract documentation

In practice, most agentic systems use all four in combination: JSON Schema for the LLM-facing tool definitions, OpenAPI for HTTP tool services, AsyncAPI for event-driven agent communication channels, and optionally GraphQL for data-fetching tools. The JSON Schema in the LLM tool definition and the OpenAPI spec for the backing HTTP service should be kept in sync — they describe the same interface at different levels of abstraction.
Security & Reliability
API contract documentation intersects with security in several important ways in agentic systems.
First, the OpenAPI
securitySchemes

section is where authentication and authorization requirements are formally documented. Every tool endpoint that requires authentication must declare its security scheme, whether API key, OAuth 2.0 Bearer token, or mutual TLS. The LLM-facing tool description must document what credentials are required and how they are passed, so the agent can correctly include them in tool invocations. Undocumented security requirements are a common source of tool call failures in production.

Second, schema validation at the tool server is a security control, not just a correctness check. An LLM that has been manipulated (e.g., via prompt injection in retrieved content) may generate tool arguments that attempt SQL injection, path traversal, or parameter pollution. Strict schema validation that enforces type, format, length, and pattern constraints on every input is a defense layer against these attacks. The contract literally specifies the boundary of acceptable inputs.

Third, versioned contracts improve reliability by making changes deliberate and detectable. A tool that changes behavior without a contract update is a reliability hazard — consumers have no way to know they need to update. When breaking changes are tracked in a version registry and flagged in CI, the risk of silent regressions is dramatically reduced.

Fourth, avoid including sensitive information — API keys, internal IP addresses, PII field names that reveal internal data structure — in publicly accessible documentation portals. The documentation portal should be accessible to the developers who need it; if it is publicly accessible, review what information the spec reveals about your internal architecture.

For reliability, define and document rate limits, timeout behavior, and retry semantics in the spec. An agent that retries a non-idempotent tool call indefinitely can cause data corruption; one that doesn't retry a transient 503 may fail unnecessarily. The contract should specify the
Retry-After
header semantics, which status codes are retryable (503, 429) versus not (400, 401), and maximum response times the agent should expect.
Mental Model

Think of an API contract as a binding legal agreement between a tool provider and its consumers — except that one of the consumers is an LLM that reads the contract at inference time to decide what arguments to construct.

In a conventional software system, an API contract is primarily for human developers who read it and write client code accordingly. In an agentic system, the contract serves a dual audience: human developers who build and maintain the integration, and the LLM that generates tool invocation arguments dynamically at runtime. This dual-audience requirement changes how you write descriptions: they must be precise enough to guide model reasoning, not just informative enough for human understanding.
Another useful mental model is to think of the contract as a
living executable specification

. Unlike a static README, the contract is consumed by validators, code generators, test runners, and CI pipelines. If the contract drifts from the implementation, the tools that consume the contract produce incorrect outputs — wrong client code, failing tests, misleading documentation. Keeping the contract as an executable artifact (generated from code, validated against traffic, diffed in CI) prevents this drift mechanically rather than relying on developer discipline.
Finally, think about versioning as semantic promises. When you publish a
v1
contract and an agent is deployed against it, you are promising that any future
v1
deployment will behave compatibly. A
v2

deployment is a new promise to new consumers who explicitly opt in. This model lets old and new consumers coexist during migration windows, which is essential in long-running agentic systems that cannot have coordinated downtime.
Key Takeaways

API contracts are executable specifications: they drive validation, code generation, testing, and documentation — not just human-readable prose. In agentic systems, the LLM is a first-class consumer of the contract at inference time.
Write
description
fields for the model, not the developer: every tool parameter and response field needs a description that guides the LLM's reasoning about when and how to populate it.
Use OpenAPI 3.x for synchronous HTTP tool interfaces and AsyncAPI 3.x for event-driven agent communication channels. These are complementary, not competing: most agentic systems need both.

Prevent breaking changes with automated detection in CI (oasdiff, bump.sh). Enforce semantic versioning: bump the major version for any change that removes, renames, or alters the type of an existing field.

Consumer-driven contract tests (Pact) let agents and tool servers be developed and deployed independently. The Pact Broker publishes agent expectations; provider CI runs verify the implementation satisfies them.
Generate specs from code wherever possible (FastAPI + Pydantic, decorators, docstrings). This eliminates spec drift mechanically, which is more reliable than developer discipline.
Schema validation at the tool server is a security control: enforce type, format, length, and pattern constraints on LLM-generated arguments to defend against prompt injection and malformed inputs.

Document error responses, rate limits, and retry semantics in the contract. An agent that doesn't know which errors are retryable and how long to wait will either over-retry (causing cascading failures) or under-retry (failing unnecessarily).
